C++ example 0.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a loop.
The example uses a vector and a vector<int> value.
```cpp
int process_cpp_0_0(const std::vector<int>& items) {
    int total = 0;
    for (int value : items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a vector and a vector<int> value.
```cpp
int process_cpp_0_45(const std::vector<int>& items) {
    int total = 0;
    for (int value : items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.

```cpp
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cpp
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cpp
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cpp
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cpp
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a set and a unique_ptr<Node> value.
```cpp
int process_cpp_0_1679(const std::vector<int>& items) {
    int total = 0;
    for (int value : items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is segmentation fault.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.
